meta.title: Guqin Finger-Technique (Hand-Gesture) Reference
meta.description: A categorized, cross-linked reference of guqin (古琴) finger techniques in jianzipu (减字谱) tablature. Each card is self-contained: the full character, pinyin, the handdrawn reduced glyph, a plain-language instruction, demonstration photo/video where available, the related techniques to compare, and the traditional poetic gesture-image where one exists.
meta.note: Explanations synthesized from Pei-You Chang (peiyouqin.com), John Thompson (silkqin.com) and Chinese-language qin sources. Demonstration photos and handdrawn notation glyphs reproduced from peiyouqin.com; gesture woodblocks from silkqin.com. See assets/photos/README.txt and resources.json for attribution.

categories.title: Notation & Tone Symbols
categories.subtitle: 弦 徽 散 按 泛
categories.description: String/hui markers and the tone-type symbols (open, stopped, harmonic).

categories.title: Right-Hand Techniques
categories.subtitle: 右手指法 · book №55–114
categories.description: The plucking hand, in the order and numbering of the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index.

categories.title: Left-Hand Techniques
categories.subtitle: 左手指法 · book №115–186
categories.description: The pressing/sliding hand, in the order and numbering of the Wu Family Qin Repertoire index.

categories.title: Not in this book
categories.subtitle: additional techniques from other sources
categories.description: Techniques in our reference that do not appear in this book’s index.

techniques.instruction_text: String number, written as a Chinese numeral (一…七) in the glyph center, telling the right hand which string to pluck.
The 13 position markers. A number (一…十三) in the glyph's upper-right gives the hui; a sub-fraction (e.g. 七六 = between hui 7 and 6, or 9.5) refines it.
techniques.etymology: The hui are the harmonic/marker positions; thirteen standard hui, each space divided into ten fēn and each fēn into ten lí.

techniques.instruction_text: Open-string tone (san yin). Left hand does not touch the string. Left-hand glyph component meaning "open".
techniques.etymology: Earliest (You Lan, Tang): the left hand does not press at all — the open, “scattered” string.

techniques.instruction_text: Stopped/pressed tone (an yin). Finger presses the string fully to the board.
techniques.etymology: “Press”: place the finger at the playing position and press the string down evenly — the general term for left-hand stopping.

techniques.instruction_text: Harmonic (fan yin). 泛起 fàn qǐ marks the start of a harmonics passage, 泛止 fàn zhǐ its end. Finger lightly touches the string over a hui.
techniques.notes: 泛起…泛止 bracket a harmonics section.
techniques.etymology: Earliest (You Lan, Tang): a left finger lightly touches at a hui for a clear harmonic; any of the four can play one.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your index finger to pluck the string.
techniques.etymology: Index finger sounding inward, using half fingernail and half flesh — strike heavy, enter light.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your index finger to strum the string. When playing, slightly bend the index finger and put your index finger tip against your thumb nail tip to strum the string. When using Muǒ and Tiaǒ, be sure your elbow is hanging flat and do not against your wrist on the instrument.
techniques.etymology: Index finger outward; the finger hangs and falls to produce a strong, clear tone.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your middle finger to pluck the string. Slightly bend the middle finger, and put more strength on the finger tip. After pluck the string, stay your middle finger on the next string and not move away yet.
techniques.poetic_image: Lone duck looks back at the flock (孤鶩顧群)
techniques.etymology: Middle finger inward, striking straight (not brushing); the last joint must fall past and under the thumbnail.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your middle finger to strum the string. Gou and Ti are a little bit stronger than Muǒ and Tiaǒ. Also keep your elbow hanging flat.
techniques.etymology: Middle finger outward (like tiāo); not too deep, or the tone is blocked.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your thumb to pluck the string. Bò and Tuo are used more often on the 6th and the 7th strings.
techniques.notes: Outward is the modern reading; see historical in/out note on B7.
techniques.etymology: Thumb outward, toward the bridge; etymology “to pull,” used when the reach is too far for the index or middle fingers.

techniques.instruction_text: Thumb plucks inward.
techniques.notes: Historic in/out confusion between 擘/劈 and 托 — see footnote 11 on https://www.silkqin.com/02qnpu/05tydq/ty3.htm. Inward is the modern reading.
techniques.etymology: Thumb inward, toward the body; rarely used. Yan Tianchi and Xu Qingshan historically reversed the senses of bò and tuō.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your ring finger to pluck the string.
techniques.etymology: Ring finger inward, mostly on strings 1–2; despite literally meaning “to strike,” dǎ is not played with force.

techniques.instruction_text: Using your ring finger to strum the string. The ring finger has less power, therefore it has to practice more to get strength. Dă and Zhai are lighter than Muǒ, Tiaǒ, Gou and Ti.
techniques.etymology: Ring finger outward (like tī, but without the thumb beneath); mostly used within gǔn and lún.

techniques.instruction_text: The combination of Muǒ and Tiaǒ, the index finger plucks the string first then strums.
The combination of Gou and Ti, the middle finger plucks the string first then strums.
RH thumb away then in
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: RH ring in then away
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Same as Muǒ Gou but faster.
Opposite of dié juān
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: dié juān, 2nd note lighter
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Successive/linked juan.
On the same string, use Ti Muǒ Tiaǒ to play a fast continuing 3 sounds. Use the tip of the nails, don't put them too deep into the string.
techniques.etymology: Bèi-suǒ (背銷): three sounds on one string with tī, mǒ, tiāo in sequence; also called shào-suǒ.

techniques.instruction_text: On the same string, do Muǒ Gou first, a slowly and gradually continuing sound, then following with Ti Muǒ Tiaǒ, a faster continuing sound.
techniques.etymology: Duǎn-suǒ (短銷): five sounds — mǒ and gōu, then a bèi-suǒ added.

techniques.instruction_text: On the same string, do the Muǒ Tiaǒ, Muǒ Gou 4 sound first then follow with the Tì Muǒ Tiaǒ 3 continuing sound.
techniques.etymology: Cháng-suǒ (長銷): seven (or nine) sounds — mǒ, tiāo, mǒ, gōu, then a bèi-suǒ added.

techniques.instruction_text: On the same string, use the ring finger, middle finger and index finger to do Zhai, Ti, Tiaǒ 3 continuing sound. When playing this technique, bend the 3 fingers slightly, then strum the string gradually. Try to make it strong and clear.
techniques.etymology: Three successive sounds on a string with zhāi, tī, tiāo in sequence; the bending knuckles line up close together.

techniques.instruction_text: Same technique as Luń but one sound less than Luń. On the same string, use either Zhai Ti or Ti Tiaǒ. Notation: (or 口一)
techniques.etymology: Two sounds using only zhāi and tī — the lún method with the index finger left out.

techniques.instruction_text: Index-middle-ring roll in
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Rú Yi literally means "as one." When there are 2 strings that can be created an Àn Yin and a Săn Yin with the same sound at one time, use the middle finger or index finger (usually use the middle finger) to strum the 2 strings together.
techniques.etymology: Two strings — one pressed, one open — sounded together using tī on both, so they read “as one.”

techniques.instruction_text: tiāo two strings as one
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Same as Rú Yi but play twice. Before strumming the string, position the index and middle finger above the thumb, do Ti then Tiaǒ. Some tablature will indicate a "Shuang Tán" follow by a "Rú Yi," which reminds the player that making the two previous sound as one sound.
techniques.etymology: Double-play, also called gǔ (鼓): two sounds on one or two strings using tī then tiāo.

techniques.instruction_text: Three snapped strokes out
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Quán fú (full fú) is a four-note connected figure: the index finger does mǒ across two adjacent strings in one motion, then immediately the middle finger does gōu across the same two strings, while the ring finger lightly stops the residual sound of the first string. The result is four soft, connected sounds that flow as one gesture.
techniques.etymology: Three strings sounded together (one sound each) with dǎ, gōu, mǒ; used with harmonics — attested as early as the You Lan.

techniques.instruction_text: Bàn fú (half fú) is the abbreviated form of quán fú: the index finger does mǒ on the two strings and the middle finger does gōu on the lower of the two strings, producing fewer (typically two) connected soft sounds rather than the full four. It is the lighter, shorter partner of quán fú.
techniques.etymology: Two strings sounded together with gōu and mǒ; also called lōu-yuán (摟圓), used only with harmonics.

techniques.instruction_text: Index+middle pluck two together
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Bō and là are an inseparable pair (撥剌). Bō: the index, middle and ring fingers are cupped together and sweep INWARD across several strings; là sweeps back OUTWARD. In practice bō is played lighter and là follows heavier, giving a strong two-stroke flourish.
techniques.notes: Often paired with 剌 as 撥剌 bō là.
techniques.etymology: First of the bō-là (潑刺) pair: three joined fingers sweep down-left across the strings, likened to a fish slapping its tail.

techniques.instruction_text: Là is the outward sweep of the cupped index/middle/ring fingers across several strings; it is the partner stroke to bō (inward sweep). The combined gesture 撥剌 is normally played bō-soft then là-strong as a single accented event.
techniques.etymology: Second of the bō-là pair: three fingers brush up-right, “like the tearing of silk.”

techniques.instruction_text: Do Bo first with lighter strength then Lá later with strong strength.
techniques.etymology: Bō-là / pō-cì (潑刺): pō (three joined fingers, down-left) then cì (reversed, up-right) — likened to a wandering fish slapping its tail.

techniques.instruction_text: Ring plucks first, then bō là
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: After doing Lá or Bo Lá , flatten together of the index, middle and ring finger and place them on the strings to stop the sound.
techniques.etymology: An abrupt closing sound: the palm covers the strings between hui 4 and 5, a clipped sound like tearing silk; not used near hui 1.

techniques.instruction_text: Same as fú
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: After bō là then fú
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Same as pāi shā
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Middle then index quickly
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Play shuāng tán strongly
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Using the index finger to strum (Tiaǒ) and the middle finger to pluck (Gou) two strings together. The strength must be the same.
techniques.etymology: Literally “to pinch”: two strings sounded at once with gōu+tiāo. Earliest (You Lan, Tang): the ring finger and thumb in unison.

techniques.instruction_text: Using the thumb and middle finger (Tuo, Gou) to pluck two strings(either the 1st and 6th or the 2nd and 7th) at the same time.The strength must be the same.
The opposite of Cuo, usually used after Cuo. Using the thumb and middle finger (Bò, Ti) or the index and middle finger (Muǒ, Ti) to strum and pluck two strings together.
techniques.etymology: Reversed cuō: where cuō uses gōu+tiāo, the reverse uses mǒ+tī — one sound from two strings.

techniques.instruction_text: Strike two pressed + one open
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Thumb pulls string up (slap)
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Thumb+index pull string up (slap)
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Use Dă, Tiăo or Gou, Tiăo back and forth on 2 strings and consisting of six notes. First Dă, then Tiăo, a slight pause, then rapidly repeat the same movement twice. Fingers must be flexible to create continuous sound.
techniques.etymology: Seven sounds on two strings alternating tiāo/gōu pairs; the art lies in the fast rhythm of the central four sounds.

techniques.instruction_text: The index finger lightly and rapidly strums out 2 or 3 strings in succession.
techniques.etymology: Index finger connecting tiāo across several adjacent strings; also called dù (度).

techniques.instruction_text: Like lì but slower
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: lì 7th→2nd string (harmonics)
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: lì or gǔn slowly, staccato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Gǔn and fú form the pair 滾拂. Gǔn: the ring finger sweeps OUTWARD from a high string down toward string 1; fú: the index finger sweeps INWARD from string 1 up toward string 7. Played together they make a continuous circular wash of sound.
techniques.notes: Often paired with 拂 as 滾拂 gǔn fú.
techniques.etymology: Etymology “to roll”: continuous rolling with the ring finger doing zhāi from string 7 toward 1; also called lín (臨), often with harmonics.

techniques.instruction_text: Fú is the inward index-finger sweep (mǒ across many strings, low to high) that partners gǔn (outward ring-finger sweep, high to low). The combination 滾拂 runs left-inside to right-outside and back, like a circle, producing a flowing rolled texture.
techniques.etymology: Fú (沸): continuous mǒ swept across the strings — smooth and flowing, never explosive; the complement of gǔn, together 滾拂.

techniques.instruction_text: The combination of Guěn and Fú. Started from the left inside to the right outside and back to the left inside, like a circle.
techniques.etymology: Gǔn-fú (滾沸): gǔn circles inside-out, then fú circles out and back inside — one continuous circular figure.

techniques.instruction_text: Play gǔn fú continuously
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Ring on marker, thumb strike, cuō
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: A two-hand cadential cluster: the left thumb does 罨 then 掐起 while the right hand does cuō, repeated, yielding three pinched sounds. A signature ending gesture in many pieces.
techniques.etymology: Tāo-cuō-sān-shēng (搯撮三聲): eight sounds combining left-hand tāo with right-hand cuō (three tāo, two cuō), each tāo carrying an implicit hammer-on.

techniques.instruction_text: qiā qǐ, fǎn chā qǐ, bō là
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Two sounds every other string
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Two sounds every third string
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Pull/pluck adjacent strings in
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Thumb. Presses with the side of the thumb where nail meets skin, or the side of the knuckle.
techniques.etymology: The thumb — the “chief finger” (after Mencius); the first finger of the hand.

techniques.instruction_text: Index finger. Used mostly for harmonics (light touch); sometimes with the thumb.
techniques.etymology: The index finger; per the Zuo-chuan, an allusion to Zi-gong’s twitching “eating finger” that foretold a feast.

techniques.instruction_text: Middle finger. Used more often on the 1st string.
techniques.etymology: The third or “leading” finger of the hand.

techniques.instruction_text: Ring finger. Presses with the left side where nail meets skin — not the fingertip. Thumb should not rise.
techniques.etymology: The ring finger; its name comes from Mencius’s “no-name finger” (wú-míng zhǐ), the nameless finger.

techniques.instruction_text: "Kneeling" ring finger — press with the back of the nail / back of the first knuckle, usually above the 5th hui.
techniques.notes: Written with a kneel mark.
techniques.etymology: A posture in which both joints of the ring finger bend so the last digit “kneels” down onto the string.

techniques.instruction_text: Chuò is an up-glide INTO the note: the finger starts roughly a hui below the target and slides up so the note arrives with a 'hidden head' (no separate attack). It pairs with zhù, the down-glide; both blend pitches smoothly, unlike 上/下 which sound two distinct pitches.
techniques.etymology: Ascending glissando: from just below the position, the slanted finger presses and slides up into the sounding spot.

techniques.instruction_text: Zhù is a down-glide INTO the note: the finger starts about a hui above the target and slides down into it like flowing water, the note emerging without a separate attack. Chuò = slide up into note, zhù = slide down into note.
techniques.etymology: Descending glissando: from just above the position, the slanted finger presses and slides down into the sounding spot.

techniques.instruction_text: Yín is a vibrato made by oscillating the stopping finger around the note position after the pluck: it begins downward then returns up, repeated 2–3 times in a narrow, gentle wave that fades. It is the lighter, smaller-amplitude vibrato.
techniques.poetic_image: Likened to a cicada singing in autumn
techniques.notes: See yin-vs-nao distinction: https://peiyouqin.com/yinandnao.html

media.caption: Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (1/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (2/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (3/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (4/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (5/5), peiyouqin.com

techniques.etymology: Vibrato: after the note, the finger oscillates nimbly about 4–5 times before returning — yín gives the note a “living pulse.”

techniques.instruction_text: A drawn-out vibrato movement. The frequency of up and down is several times more than Yín. The total number can be 7 to 12 times of the frequency.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.
techniques.etymology: An extended yín — a vibrato sustained for a long time.

techniques.instruction_text: A calm vibrato. It is rocking the string back and force without moving the finger.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.
techniques.etymology: “Fixed” vibrato: a slight motion without leaving the position, “nailing” the surface to keep the sound alive.

techniques.instruction_text: Up glissando with vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Down glissando with vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: A thin vibrato movement, more delicate than Yín.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.
techniques.etymology: A subtle yín, its oscillation fine and minute.

techniques.instruction_text: Swinging vibrato. Similar to Shuang Zhùang (see Shuang Zhuang on next page) but slower.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.
techniques.etymology: “Roaming” vibrato: a chuò, then a descending retreat and a chuò back up — two sounds; also called dàng-yín.

techniques.instruction_text: Slow stressed vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: A yín (vibrato) played with quickness and urgency.
techniques.etymology: A yín played with quickness and urgency.

techniques.instruction_text: A yín played slowly and moderately — the calm counterpart of jí yín.
techniques.etymology: A yín played in a slow, moderate fashion.

techniques.instruction_text: Vibrato, less finger movement
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Slight / brief yin.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.

techniques.instruction_text: 1st vibrato slower than 2nd
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Double vibrato: on the same string, play twice, using yín each time.
techniques.etymology: Double vibrato — play twice on the same string, with yín both times.

techniques.instruction_text: Immediately vibrato. Do Yín as soon as the left hand presses the string and the right hand plays the string.
techniques.notes: Variation of 吟 yín.
techniques.etymology: “Fallen-finger” vibrato: the finger falls onto the string and immediately plays yín.

techniques.instruction_text: “Flying” vibrato: move up once and down twice (or up twice and down twice); read the tablature for the exact pattern.
techniques.etymology: “Flying” vibrato — up once and down twice (or up twice, down twice).

techniques.instruction_text: Náo is the larger, more forceful vibrato: the stopping finger swings up then down through a WIDER arc than yín, repeated 2–3 times, strong at first and fading. Rule of thumb: yín oscillates around the note in a small wave (begins downward), náo in a broad, vigorous wave (begins upward).
techniques.poetic_image: Behold the howling ape, climbing the tall tree; wishing to go up yet not up, its momentum pressing on (瞻彼號猿，升於喬木。欲上不上，其勢逐逐)
techniques.poetic_verse: 瞻彼號猿，升於喬木。欲上不上，其勢逐逐
techniques.notes: Some teachers pronounce/relate this to 揉 róu.

media.caption: Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (1/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (2/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (3/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (4/5), peiyouqin.com
Yín vs Náo — early-source comparison (5/5), peiyouqin.com

techniques.etymology: A wider, slower vibrato exceeding the position by ~2–3 fēn; larger than yín, lending the note an ancient strength.

techniques.instruction_text: The movement is the same as Náo but the timing of the vibrato is longer. Same situation as Cháng Yín.
techniques.notes: Variation of 猱 náo.

techniques.instruction_text: Up glissando with heavy vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Slow stressed heavy vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Slightly Náo. For a further study on distinguish the differences between Yín and Náo and a short film of the demo,
techniques.notes: Variation of 猱 náo.

techniques.instruction_text: Same situation as Lùo Zhĭ Yín. Do Náo as soon as the left hand presses the string and the right hand plays the string.
techniques.notes: Variation of 猱 náo.
techniques.etymology: “Fallen-finger” wide vibrato, performed like luò-zhǐ-yín.

techniques.instruction_text: A náo played in a large, broad, slow fashion.
techniques.etymology: A náo played in a large, broad, slow fashion.

techniques.instruction_text: A fast Náo. Feels tight and rapid but not in a hurry.
techniques.notes: Variation of 猱 náo.
techniques.etymology: A náo played with urgency and speed.

techniques.instruction_text: Down glissando, heavy vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: "Strike-nao" — a nao combined with a zhuang accent; common in early handbooks (e.g. Shen Qi Mi Pu).
techniques.notes: Variation of 猱 náo.
techniques.etymology: Combines náo and zhuàng; the motion stays above the current position, never below it.

techniques.instruction_text: Articulated subdued bigger vibrato
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Zhuàng (strike-against) is done AFTER the right hand has plucked: the stopping finger snaps very quickly up (rightward) by roughly 1/5 to 1/2 of a hui-position and snaps back to the original spot, giving a sharp accent ornament. The key contrast with dòu is timing — zhuàng happens after the pluck.
techniques.etymology: “Knock”: at the pressed spot, quickly move up slightly then snap back for another note, played suddenly.

techniques.instruction_text: "Double strike against" — perform zhuang twice.
Small-range zhuàng, high register
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Same technique as "Zhàng" but moves the left hand very quickly down first (to the left) about 1/5 - 1/2 of to the next "Hui" position and moves back quickly to the spot indicated. It is like a faster motion of Tuì fù.
宋成玉礀[琴書大全]: 注少許,略作猱, 而復引少許. Slides down over to the hui position a little bit, then slightly náo (once or twice), and then slides up to above the hui position a little bit.
techniques.notes: Also written 喚 / 換.
techniques.etymology: “Call-out”: on a downward move, go back up then quickly down again — like zhuàng reversed, giving two sounds.

techniques.instruction_text: Dòu is almost identical in motion to zhuàng (a quick up-and-back of the stopping finger), but it is performed SIMULTANEOUSLY with the right-hand pluck rather than after it, so the ornament is fused into the attack of the note. Timing is the sole distinguishing feature from zhuàng.
techniques.etymology: “Bump”: at the end of a chuò, smoothly add a bump back to the original pitch — method like zhuàng but quick, delicate and quiet.

techniques.instruction_text: While the right hand plucks the string that the left hand has pressed down, the left hand glides up to the spot that is indicated. The pressing and moving of the left hand should be solid so that it will create a very clear sound. If there is one ascending after another ascending, the notation will be "二上" (Èr Shàng , up twice). In Guqin tableture, only the final destination of Èr Shàng is indicated. So the player has to listen to the tone and move his or her finger up to a proper position for the first ascending tone. Each ascending tone is approximately one whole step, for example, Do- Re- Mi, or Re-Mi-Sol, or Mi- Sol- La, or Sol-La-Do, or La-Do-Re.
techniques.etymology: Any move toward the bridge (“to go up”): after playing, the finger slides up one position for a new sound.

techniques.instruction_text: opposite of "Shàng." While the right hand plucks the string that the left hand has pressed down, the left hand glides down to the spot that it is indicated. If there is one descending after another descending, the notation will be "二下" (Èr Xià, down twice). Same as Èr Shàng that the tableture only indicate the final desitination. So the player has to listen to the tone and move his or her finger down to a proper position for the first descending tone. Each descending tone is approximately one whole step. For example, Do- La- Sol, or La- Sol- Mi, or Sol- Mi-Re, or Mi- Re- Do, or Re- Do- La. The Variations of Yín and Náo:
techniques.etymology: Any move toward the nut (“to go down”): after playing, the finger slides down one position for a new sound.

techniques.instruction_text: Slide right and lift off
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Slide up 3–4 places, lift off
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Down glissando with yín/náo
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Tǎng is essentially a slow 下 (xià): after the note sounds, the left hand glides downward (lower pitch) but more slowly and gently than a normal xià, giving a relaxed descending portamento.
techniques.etymology: “Flowing ascent”: while moving up with the final sound not yet finished, continue the sound and push off back down.

techniques.instruction_text: 往來得聲自上而下三次(或兩次). When a finger of the left hand presses down a string and after the right hand has pulled the string, the left hand moves down to the next sound position to the left and then moves back to where it started; and repeats this movement twice or three times to produce a total of 4 or 6 sounds. (ex. 5,3,5,3,5,3).
techniques.etymology: “Back-and-forth”: e.g. play at hui 9, slide up to hui 8 and play yín, then return — essentially a doubled jìn-fù.

techniques.instruction_text: "Advance" — slide up one position (component of jinfu).
techniques.etymology: “Advance”: to ascend one position with a sound after a note.

techniques.instruction_text: "Retreat" — slide down one position.
techniques.etymology: “Withdraw”: to descend one position with a sound after a note.

techniques.instruction_text: Return after jìn or tuì
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Jìn fù (advance-and-return): after the right hand plucks a stopped note, the left hand glides UP (rightward, higher pitch) to a target position then glides back to the start, producing three sounds (one plucked, two slid) — one solid and two soft.
techniques.etymology: “Advance-return”: from the pressed spot ascend one position with a sound, then descend back with a sound — two sounds on one string.

techniques.instruction_text: jìn fù in a subdued manner
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Tuì fù (retreat-and-return) is the mirror of jìn fù: after the pluck the left hand glides DOWN (leftward, lower pitch) to the target then returns to the start. Same three-sound, one-solid-two-soft structure, opposite direction.
techniques.etymology: “Withdraw-return”: from the pressed spot descend one position with a sound, then ascend back — two sounds on one string.

techniques.instruction_text: When a finger of the left hand presses down a string and after the right hand has pulled the string, the left hand glides up to the next Hui position to the right; and then while the right hand pulls the string again, the left hand glides back to where it started, as the action of "Zhù."
techniques.etymology: “Divide-open”: on one string, play at the pressed spot, move up for a sound, return and play again — three sounds.

techniques.instruction_text: Qiā qǐ is the thumb's version of dài qǐ: after the thumb stops a string and a finger presses the same string a position lower, the thumbnail plucks/lifts the string upward to sound an added note without the right hand. Pairs with 罨 in 掐撮三聲.
techniques.notes: Common in beginner pieces (e.g. 古琴吟).
techniques.etymology: The left thumb and ring finger are placed on the string, then the thumb hooks upward to sound it (a left-hand pluck).

techniques.instruction_text: "Claw up" — left finger plucks the string upward to sound it.
techniques.etymology: “Claw-pull”: the thumb pulls off at the playing position to sound the open string.

techniques.instruction_text: Dài qǐ: after the left ring finger has stopped and the string has sounded, the ring fingertip flicks/lifts the string as it leaves, producing an added open-string (sǎn) sound with no right-hand pluck. Contrast 掐起 (thumb) and 抓起 (thumb, lighter).
techniques.etymology: “Lead-pull”: the ring finger takes the string and pulls off for one open-string sound.

techniques.instruction_text: Yǎn: with NO right-hand pluck, a left-hand finger (commonly thumb, middle or ring) sharply taps/hammers the string onto the surface to produce a low, dull, dark note. It is the hammer-on counterpart to the pull-off techniques 掐起/帶起.
techniques.etymology: “Cover”: a left finger presses a string to sound it without a right-hand pluck — thumb, middle or ring quickly striking the string onto the face.

techniques.instruction_text: This technique is mostly executed with the middle or ring finger and sometimes the thumb. Same technique as Yǎn but without pressing down any string before doing Yǎn.
techniques.etymology: “Empty cover”: a yǎn made when the string has not been plucked first — the same motion as yǎn.

techniques.instruction_text: “Xu” literally means “empty, unfilled,” therefore a “Xu Zhuàng “is to have a Zhuàng technique happen after a non- plucked sound. For example, the left hand may do a Zhuàng after an upward moving technique (Shàng or Jìng ), or a downward moving technique (Xià or Fù ) or a vibrato technique (Yín or Náo ) has been performed.
techniques.etymology: “Empty knock”: a zhuàng made where the right hand has not plucked — the sound from the left-hand motion alone.

techniques.instruction_text: This technique is particularly used on the 1st string for the middle finger of the left hand. After the middle finger presses a sting down, it makes the 1st string sound by pushing it outward.
techniques.notes: Common in beginner pieces (e.g. 古琴吟).
techniques.etymology: “Push-out”: only the middle finger, only on string 1 — the last two digits straighten and push the string out, releasing it to sound.

techniques.instruction_text: LH release without leaving board
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Two sounds as a chord
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Related to 應合 (yìng hé): after stopping and sounding a string, the left hand releases/slides to a new position so that a following right-hand stroke on another string meets it at a matching (usually unison or octave) pitch — the two notes joining together.
techniques.etymology: “Release-harmonize”: the ring finger pulls off for a sound, then quickly presses a second string for the right hand, the two harmonizing as one.

techniques.instruction_text: LH thumb lift as ring plays next
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: This technique is creating a kind of chord. The left hand plucks one string (can be the middle finger Tuīchū, or the ring finger Dàiqǐ or the thumb Zhuāqǐ), at the same time, the right hand plucks another string to make both strings sound together.
techniques.etymology: “Same-sound”: two strings sounded in different ways are aligned so closely they read as a single sound.

techniques.instruction_text: Same as tóng shēng
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: The left middle or ring finger presses down a string, and the right hand plucks it, the left hand stays on the same string and does not move away yet. While the right hand plucks another string, the left hand moves either up or down to the position where it has the same sound as the string that the right hand had played. Eventually making both strings sound together (one is a solid sound, the other is a soft sound).
techniques.etymology: “Response-harmonize”: after pressing and playing, the finger moves up or down so its sound responds to an open string played by the right hand.

techniques.instruction_text: Pressed note, wait, slide forward
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: LH stays for RH to play later
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: LH holds strings, RH gǔn
techniques.notes: From the Wu Family Qin Repertoire (虞山吳氏琴譜) index; not in the primary peiyouqin/silkqin sources.

techniques.instruction_text: Multiple uninterrupted sounds made with tiāo on the pressed strings, ringing out “like a string of bells.”
techniques.etymology: Multiple uninterrupted sounds using tiāo successively on the pressed strings; etymology “like a string of bells” — the gesture-image of shaking a rope of bells.

techniques.instruction_text: A standard three-cuō cadential figure: two strings are pinched (cuō) together, alternated with a hammer-on/pull-off ornament, then repeated to make three pinched sounds. Common at phrase endings.
A linked double-pluck (mo+gou feel) on adjacent strings; compare quanfu/banfu.
techniques.etymology: In the You Lan, juān runs the index finger quickly inward across the strings — a rapid double inward stroke; the earliest attestation.

techniques.instruction_text: The combination of Muǒ and Gou, on the same string, do Muǒ first then Gou, it creates 2 gradually continuing sounds.
"Lift/rise" — general term for left-hand-initiated sound; appears as a component in qiaqi, zhuaqi, daiqi.
techniques.etymology: “Rise”: a pull-off element used in compounds such as dài-qǐ, tāo-qǐ and fàn-qǐ.

techniques.instruction_text: Tuō ('tuō shàng') is essentially a slow 上 (shàng): after the note sounds, the left hand glides upward (higher pitch) more slowly than a normal shàng, a leisurely ascending portamento. It is the up-glide counterpart of tǎng.
techniques.notes: Also written 拖上 tuō shàng.
techniques.etymology: Drag: at the end the ring finger slides up one position — a sound likened to a cicada crying from a tree.

techniques.instruction_text: “Dig-pull”: the ring finger presses the string while the thumb, at a higher position, uses its nail to pull the string up and sound it.
techniques.etymology: “Dig-pull” — ring finger presses, thumb pulls up with the nail to sound the string; comes in fundamental and separated-position types.
